qa_pairs.question: What external resources are available for technical writing guidance?
qa_pairs.answer: You can find online self-guided Technical Writing courses from Google and a Documentation guide from Write the Docs.

qa_pairs.question: Where should I look for style inspiration if I'm unsure about layout?
qa_pairs.answer: If in doubt, imitate existing pages on docs.alliancecan.ca, or look for inspiration on Wikipedia.

qa_pairs.question: How should graphic design be handled in relation to content on the Wiki?
qa_pairs.answer: Graphic design should be separated from content as much as possible. Avoid using extra line breaks, tabs, spaces, or extra spaces after sentences for vertical spacing or paragraph indentation. Style adjustments will be made universally using stylesheets and templates.

qa_pairs.question: What is the recommended approach for including screenshots in Wiki pages?
qa_pairs.answer: Screenshots are helpful, especially in how-tos and tutorials. Full-sized screenshots should float to the right-hand side and be scaled down to avoid interrupting the text flow. If important information becomes unreadable, consider using a cropped picture or reminding the reader in the caption that they can 'Click on the image for a larger version.'

qa_pairs.question: How should blank lines be used at the end of sections?
qa_pairs.answer: Leave one blank line at the end of each section before the following header, as the translation package uses this for determining translation unit boundaries.

qa_pairs.question: How should links to other pages or sites be displayed?
qa_pairs.answer: Links should have a human-oriented description for display rather than showing the raw URL.

qa_pairs.question: What are the capitalization rules for titles and headings?
qa_pairs.answer: Capitalize only the first word and proper nouns in titles and headings. Following Wikipedia, we prefer APA sentence case for all titles, including page titles.

qa_pairs.question: Are there specific templates available for use on the Wiki?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, there are multiple templates available. Of particular interest are templates for including a command within the Wiki and for including a source code file within the Wiki.

qa_pairs.question: What is the general process for translating a Wiki page?
qa_pairs.answer: First, the content is written in the original language. Second, the page is marked for translation. Then, a human translates the page using tools from the Wiki extension 'Translate'. Finally, a second human reviews the translation.

qa_pairs.question: What happens if a page has not yet been translated or reviewed?
qa_pairs.answer: If a page has not yet been translated, users will see it in the original language. If a translation has not yet been reviewed, users will see the non-reviewed translation.

qa_pairs.question: What are translation units and why are they used?
qa_pairs.answer: Translation units are small segments like a title, paragraph, or image that the translation extension splits the page content into. They allow individual units to be translated, so a small modification doesn't require re-translating the entire page, and they help track translation progress.

qa_pairs.question: What are the steps to mark a new page for translation?
qa_pairs.answer: To mark a new page for translation, you should: 1) change the original language to French on Special:PageLanguage if applicable, 2) enclose translatable content within <translate> tags, 3) enclose code blocks in </translate><translate> tags to exclude them, 4) try to exclude wiki markup, 5) ensure the <languages /> tag is at the very top, 6) go to 'View' mode and click 'Mark this page for translation', 7) review translation units, 8) set 'fr' or 'en' as the priority language, and 9) click 'Mark this version for translation'.

qa_pairs.question: What should I avoid doing when making changes to a page that has already been translated?
qa_pairs.answer: You must not change the automatically generated tags of the form <!--T:3--> when making changes to a page that has already been translated.

qa_pairs.question: How do I mark changes to an existing, translated page for translation?
qa_pairs.answer: To mark changes, you should: 1) enclose new text to be translated within <translate> tags, 2) exclude code blocks using </translate><translate> tags, 3) exclude wiki markup, 4) go to 'View' mode and click 'marked for translation' from the 'This page has changes since it was last marked for translation' message, 5) review translation units, 6) verify 'fr' or 'en' as the priority language, and 7) click 'Mark this version for translation'.

qa_pairs.question: When should I select the 'Do not invalidate translations' checkbox?
qa_pairs.answer: You should only select the 'Do not invalidate translations' checkbox if the change is minor, such as a typo, and doesn't require the other-language versions to be adjusted.

qa_pairs.question: Are code blocks included in the translation process by human translators?
qa_pairs.answer: No, the documentation team has instructed human translators to exclude code blocks from translation because they are not programmers and cannot distinguish between code and comments in all languages.

qa_pairs.question: What are the suggestions for handling comments in code blocks that won't be translated?
qa_pairs.answer: To address comments in untranslated code blocks, you can: 1) move the information from comments into the surrounding text which will be translated, 2) use index comments like 'NOTE 1' to link external text to relevant code, or 3) if bilingual and familiar with the translation apparatus, translate the code block yourself. It's important to remember that comments will not normally be translated and to consider how this affects user understanding.

qa_pairs.question: How do I add an item to the sidebar for translation?
qa_pairs.answer: To add a translatable item to the sidebar, you need to: 1) add the new content to [[MediaWiki:Sidebar]] (e.g., as <tt>some-tag</tt> or <tt>{{(}}{{(}}int:some-tag{{)}}{{)}}</tt> for links), 2) add the tags to [[MediaWiki:Sidebar-messages]], 3) define the content of the tag in English on [[MediaWiki:some-tag]], and 4) translate the content of the tag on the specific translation page provided.
